result.title: File uploaded
result.subtitle: Share this link with the recipient. Typically, the link is valid for 48 hours.

about.title: Your files, perfectly private.
about.intro: We don't just send your file. Your file is encrypted right on your device, sliced into small chunks, and scattered across independent servers owned by different operators. No single server receives the whole file, so no one in the world can know its contents or even guess its real size.

split.title: File scattered across different operators
split.desc: The file is not stored in one place. It is cut into chunks that are sent to different independent servers. Each server's operator only sees a part of the encrypted file and cannot even guess the real size. The operator doesn't know on which other servers the rest of the chunks are located.

key.title: The key is only in the link
key.desc: Instead of the content of these chunks, the servers only see encrypted noise. The decryption key is hidden in the link itself, so only you and your recipient can open the file.

anon.title: Total anonymity
anon.desc: No accounts — no registration. When the recipient opens the link, the file pieces are downloaded and glued back together only on their device.

hood.title: A bit more technical details
hood.intro: When you send a file, the following happens:

availability.title: 1. Availability Check:
availability.desc: The browser queries enabled XFTP servers and filters out the unreachable ones.
availability.note: Note: The servers are hosted by different people. This eliminates a single point of failure.

sharding.title: 2. Fragmentation:
sharding.desc: The browser slices the file into pieces (chunks).

dispatch.title: 3. Encryption and parallel dispatch:
dispatch.desc: Each chunk is encrypted and uploaded to a random server from the pool of available servers (step 1). The upload happens in parallel.

map.title: 4. Meta-file:
map.desc: Once all chunks are uploaded, the browser creates a meta-file indicating where and in what order all chunks are located, along with the decryption keys for each chunk. This file is also encrypted and sent to a random server from the pool of available servers (step 1).

hash.title: 5. URL Hashing:
hash.desc: The key for the meta-file is placed into the hash of your link (everything after the `#` sign).
hash.note: Note: this part of the link is not sent to the server when you open the page in a browser.

about.reverse: Downloading works in reverse: the browser reads the hash, downloads the meta-file, decrypts it, downloads chunks from the servers, decrypts them, and glues them together right in the device's RAM.
